channel.title: Claude-Enriched YouTube Digest
channel.description: Insights + transcripts for curated YouTube channels, generated by youtube_digest.py

item.title: [Dwarkesh Clips] Why Don't Google's TPUs Threaten NVIDIA? – Jensen Huang
item.dc:creator: Dwarkesh Clips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

黄仁勋没说出口的那句话

表面上，这段话是在回答"TPU 会不会威胁 Nvidia"；但如果把他每一句都拆开看，他其实回答的是另一个问题——"为什么一个专用芯片永远无法取代一个通用平台？" 他几乎没有正面比较性能、价格或能效，而是把话题偷换到了"生态广度"和"可被他人操作"这两件事上。这是一个非常 Jensen 式的修辞动作：当你的对手在你擅长的维度逼近时，把讨论拉到一个他们还没想到要竞争的维度。

意外观察：Nvidia 真正卖的不是芯片，是"可被陌生人操作性"

黄仁勋在中段埋了一句很容易被忽略的话——"our computers are designed to be operated by other people"。这句话表面普通，实则是整段访谈的"隐藏主轴"。TPU 是 Google 为"Google 自己"设计的；它不需要对外部操作者友好，正如你家的厨房不需要对陌生厨师友好。而 Nvidia 的芯片从一开始就是"公共基础设施"——任何人都能接手、运营、转租。

这让我想到集装箱的历史：1956 年马尔科姆·麦克莱恩真正的发明并不是那个金属箱子（箱子早就存在），而是一套让任何港口、任何船、任何货主都能无缝交接的标准。集装箱之所以摧毁了旧航运业，不是因为它更大，而是因为它可被陌生人操作。TPU 是定制货柜，CUDA 是集装箱标准。Google 造的是更快的船，Nvidia 造的是让所有人都能上船的协议。

跨领域连接：语言学里的"通用语现象"（lingua franca）

在语言史上，拉丁语、阿拉伯语、英语之所以成为通用语，从来不是因为它们"更优雅"或"更高效"，而是因为在它们之上积累的二阶资产——法律文书、科学论文、商业合约——已经多到切换成本远超性能收益。CUDA 正处在这个位置：它本身未必是最优的张量抽象，但全世界的 PyTorch、JAX、分子动力学求解器、流体仿真代码都"长在它上面"。这就是为什么 Jensen 要反复强调 molecular dynamics、drug discovery、Lily、XAI——他在暗示：AI 热潮有一天会降温，但 CUDA 下沉为"科学计算的通用语"之后，就算 AI 退潮，它也已经是基础设施。

这里有一个更反直觉的推论：TPU 越成功，对 Nvidia 威胁反而越小。因为 TPU 越深入 Google 内部，它的专用性就越强、外溢性就越低；而 Nvidia 每多卖一张 GPU，CUDA 生态就多一个节点、多一份路径依赖。这是一个罕见的"竞争对手替你加固护城河"的结构——Google 每训练一个更强的 Gemini，都在向世界证明"Transformer 架构值得投入"，而这个投入的 90% 最终会流向……CUDA 生态。

📝 转录文本

主持人提问：

Arguably two out of the top three models in the world, Claude and Gemini, were trained on TPU. What does that mean for Nvidia going forward?

黄仁勋回答：

一、Nvidia 与 TPU 不是同一种东西

Well, we have — we built a very different thing. You know, what Nvidia built is accelerated computing, not a tensor processing unit.

And accelerated computing is used for all kinds of things — you know, molecular dynamics and quantum chromodynamics; and it's used for data processing, data frames, structured data, unstructured data; it's used for fluid dynamics, particle physics; and in addition, we use it for AI.

And so accelerated computing is much more diverse. And although AI is the conversation today — obviously very important and impactful — computing is much broader than that. What Nvidia has done is reinvented the way computing is done, from general-purpose computing to accelerated computing.

二、市场覆盖远超任何 ASIC

Our market reach is far greater than any TPU, than any ASIC can possibly have. And so if you look at our position, we're the only company that accelerates applications of all kinds. We have a gigantic ecosystem, and so all kinds of frameworks and algorithms all run on Nvidia.

三、关键差异：为"他人操作"而设计

And because our computers are designed to be operated by other people, anyone who's an operator could buy our systems.

Most of these homebuilt systems — you have to be your own operator, because it was never designed to be flexible enough for other people to operate. And so, as a result of the fact that anybody can operate our systems, we're in every cloud, including Google and Amazon and Azure and OCI.

And so, whether you want to operate it to rent, or operate it [for yourself] — if you want to operate it to rent, you better have a large ecosystem of customers in many industries that will be the offtakers. If you want to operate it for yourself, we obviously have the ability to help you operate yourself — like, for example, for Elon with XAI.

四、从 XAI 到礼来：跨行业的自运营能力

And because we could enable operators in any company, in any industry, you could use it to build a supercomputer for scientific research and drug discovery at Lilly. And so we can help them operate their own supercomputer and use it for the entire diversity of drug discovery and biological sciences that we accelerate.

五、CUDA：不只是张量处理器

And so there are just, you know, a whole bunch of applications that we can address that you can't do so with TPUs — because Nvidia's built CUDA as a fantastic tensor processing unit as well, but it does every life cycle of data processing and computing and AI, and so on, so forth.

And so our market opportunity is just a lot larger. Our reach is a lot greater. And because we basically support every application in the world now, you could build Nvidia systems anywhere and know that there will be customers for it.

And so it's a very different thing.

If you enjoyed this clip, you can watch the full episode here and subscribe for more clips. Thanks.

观看原视频 →

item.title: [a16z Deep Dives] How AI Is Entering the Physical World | Deep Dives with a16z
item.dc:creator: a16z Deep Dives
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一、最反直觉的观察：AI 不是在"学会"硬件，而是在"伪装"硬件为软件

表面上看，这是两家把 AI 带入物理世界的公司。但如果你仔细听 David 说的那句"我们建了一个编译器，让模型感觉自己在写 Python 程序而不是在设计电路板"——你会发现真正的突破根本不是 AI 理解了物理世界，而是人类把物理世界重新编码成了 AI 已经懂的语言。

这是一个极其狡猾的认知翻转：过去三十年，软件工程师一直在试图"模拟物理世界"（CAD、仿真、数字孪生）；现在这两家公司做的恰恰相反——把物理世界"软件化"，直到它能被 LLM 当作代码消化。Alex 那句"Everything is code. Everything is computer."不是口号，是方法论。

换句话说，他们不是在造物理 AI，他们是在为物理世界做"语义迁移"。数据不够？那就换一个数据充足的语言（代码）来描述问题。这是数据稀缺时代的元策略——一种智力层面的套利。

二、隐藏的系统联系：建筑业、硬件制造与 Pre-Anduril 时代的国防工业，本质上是同一种病

Alex 轻描淡写地提到"这看起来非常像 Anduril 之前的国防工业"——这是整场对话里被低估得最狠的一句话。

让我把这条暗线拉出来：

维度 传统国防 大型建设 美国 PCB 制造
资金来源 政府预算 Project Finance (求稳定 IRR) 军工合同为主
激励结构 不追求上行，只求零风险 同上 同上
结果 创新窒息 劳动生产率 50 年下降 量产能力蒸发

真正的洞察是：凡是由"风险厌恶型资本"主导的行业，都会在几十年后失去"如何建造野心"这块肌肉。 Alex 睡前闭眼看到的那条下降曲线，不是建筑业的专属现象，它是任何"下行风险不对称 > 上行收益不对称"的资本结构必然结出的果实。

所以 Diode 和 Unlimited Industries 真正在做的，不是技术升级，而是激励结构的重写——用垂直整合把自己变成"敢拿上行风险"的那一方，然后把成果当产品卖给不敢拿风险的那一方。这是 Anduril 的打法在土木和电子上的再现。

三、跨领域连接：电路板的"文化断裂"与知识工作的"第一性原理失忆症"

David 讲了一个特别刺痛人的故事：香港的工程师把板子设计得极其紧凑，因为"做板子的是我朋友，我不想让他多过一次 SMT"。美国工程师则舒舒服服地把设计发去海外，感受不到制造的疼痛。

这件事有个惊人的跨领域回声——它和大语言模型时代程序员的"调试能力退化"是同一个现象：当反馈回路被拉长、被中介化、被舒适化，设计者就会系统性地失去"手感"。

  • PCB 工程师失去 DFM 直觉 ← 因为制造被外包到地球另一端
  • 软件工程师失去调试直觉 ← 因为 Claude 一键生成，疼痛被吸收
  • 建筑师失去可建造性直觉 ← 因为设计和施工被严格的 stage-gate 切开
  • 电工、技工在美国消亡 ← 因为整个社会把"动手"外包给了服务业

这条隐藏规律是：每一次"分工效率"的提升，都会以"跨层直觉"的退化为代价。 而 AI 正在做一件出乎意料的事——它不是在加剧这种分工，它是在把所有层级重新缝合到一个 agent 的上下文窗口里。一个模型同时懂电路、懂制造、懂热仿真、懂装配——它某种意义上恢复了"作坊师傅"那种端到端的整全感，只不过以硅基形式。

这是最让我震撼的啊哈时刻：AI 在物理世界最重要的贡献，可能不是替代人类劳动，而是重新整合被现代分工撕碎的"整全直觉"。 香港工程师的那种"我朋友要做这块板"的共情，未来可能由一个同时持有设计文件和制造约束的 agent 来承担。

四、一个预测性的大胆推论

如果 David 的"仿真作为 training-time 工具而非 inference-time 工具"的判断是对的，那么整个工程仿真软件行业（Ansys、Cadence、Synopsys 这类公司）的商业模式将在 5 年内发生质变——它们的客户不再是工程师，而是训练集群。仿真的价值会从"帮人验证"转向"帮模型长出物理直觉"，就像围棋引擎从"帮人分析棋局"变成"AlphaGo 的陪练"一样。

这意味着 EDA/CAE 公司要么变成"AI 训练数据的磨坊"，要么被绕过。这条赛道的颠覆不会来自更好的仿真，而是来自把仿真作为数据工厂使用的公司。

📝 转录文本

How AI Is Entering the Physical World | Deep Dives with a16z

开场金句

"I want to be able to spin up a hardware company the same way that my friends spin up B2B SAS. Like you should be able to say I want to do something that's considered very hard and just go and do it."

"We basically built a compiler that gives the model enough hints that it feels like it's writing a Python program instead of designing a circuit boards."

"It's basically this combination of a very modelled approach that allows you to use these agents to write code which is what they know how to do — put on rails." "Everything is code."

"The last frontier standing is we don't have enough data. The data is like the thing that we need to generate as a society if we want circuit boards to be automated by AI."

"Making sure that you design the system to actually be fully autonomous and to not be human in the loop. For us at least it feels like it's driven a very different architecture."

嘉宾介绍

We're thrilled to be here today with David Asagi and Alex Moden.

  • David is the CEO of Diode Computers, and they're using AI to design and manufacture custom circuit boards faster and better than before, and faster than ever possible in the United States.
  • Alex is the CEO of Unlimited Industries, an AI-native firm that vertically integrates design, engineering, procurement and construction for big infrastructure projects.

So we're here today to talk about physical world AI. When I say that, I think a lot of people probably think about things like humanoids and robotics foundation models. But while I think robotic housekeepers folding your laundry is still a few years away — or maybe if you're really optimistic, a few months — AI is already starting to cross this chasm with use cases that move atoms.

So these companies are working on physical world AI at two very different scales, from the micro to the macro. And I'm excited to get your perspectives about where we are and what's ahead.

Alex, David, welcome to the show.

— Yeah, excited to be here. — Thank you.

一、十年内建筑业能否全自动化？

Q: Alex, you've said that in 10 years all construction will be fully automated, which feels like a pretty bold claim. What does that actually mean? What does it take to get there?

Yeah, I think it's probably helpful to level set on what a construction project looks like. It starts with a developer who's got an empty lot of land and they want to make some sort of big project there. Depending on how big the project is — if you're going to build a power plant or a hospital or some large facility — you're going to spend almost a year, sometimes a year and a half, just doing design for that.

There are hundreds of engineers that touch this. There are lots of different project managers that touch this. And it's this orchestration of mechanical and process engineers, electrical engineers, civil and structural folks, all kind of working together to do the pre-construction package — effectively a giant set of instructions that you can then hand to a general contractor or some builder who will order the things on there and actually construct the facility.

IFC 包：第一个被端到端自动化的环节

That first part is line of sight today for how we automate end to end. Typically we call that final output an IFC package — an Issued-For-Construction package — where you will literally feed in a site, a bunch of different requirements about what you're trying to build, and anything you want to stipulate about how it gets built. AI is going to explore tens of thousands of different permutations about how optimally to design that facility — at a button click. And then what you get back is a globally optimized IFC package issued for construction.

Q: Optimized for what?

It depends what the optimization function is. The easiest way to think about that might be like capex — how much does this thing cost. But a much better way to think about it is the total cost of ownership of the project. If you really care about operation and maintenance of the facility, how constructible is the facility itself?

A lot of things we generally see in industry today — there are so many different segments and slices of people that all optimize for very different things. Being able to approach designing these giant things the same way you do from a software perspective — a very parametric, super flexible approach — you can optimize on any main metric.

第二阶段：机器人接管工地

So that's the first half: how do you automate that end to end — which is what we're working on now, and have a product to do.

The second part does look like a bunch of robotics. That's everything from autonomous earth movers (which feels more tractable in the short term) to a site full of tons of humanoids and drones and all sorts of autonomous robotics.

We very much think that future is destined to happen over the next decade — and a big piece of that is if the incentives are properly aligned, which I'm happy to talk more about later.

二、电子硬件自动化的时间线

Q: David, what is the equivalent timeline for automation in hardware, and in particular electronics — both design and manufacturing?

So I have to be careful, because my timelines are getting shorter and shorter, and I need to stay on the reasonable side.

It's really interesting to hear Alex describe AI applied to construction, because I can draw very immediate parallels to hardware. For this discussion I'd like to stick to circuit boards specifically — both the design and the assembly of a circuit card.

I am reasonably confident — we do a lot of work with Anthropic, for example — that the jump we see in design capabilities between each publicly available model tier is wild. We thought it would be five years. I think I can probably say two.

The caveat: there are very different types of electronics design, and making a blanket statement about all of them isn't appropriate. But there is a subset that I really care about, and I'll go into details as to why, that I think will be fully automated in two years in terms of design.

设计之外：制造的 80/20 问题

The other reason it's interesting: for us it's not just design, it's also manufacturing. Alex said: we have this very high optimization function on what the plan looks like, and then there will be a bunch of robotics on the manufacturing side. We already have the robotics — the electronics industry has had robots for years. The biggest problem is there's an 80/20 — robotic automation versus manual labor — and right now nobody has really bridged that gap in the United States.

Q: Can you double-click on that 80/20?

Normally there's a process called surface mount technology (SMT). A robot places every single component on top of a circuit board, then you bake it in an oven and you're done.

But the 20% that is very hard to automate: some components don't fit that nice mold. Maybe it's a very big transformer that needs to be soldered with a different process. Maybe it's something that hugs the PCB. Even the assembly of the circuit board itself into an enclosure — that's usually not fully automated either.

Companies like Foxconn (for Apple) or Pegatron have solved that problem with labor, and that makes complete sense for certain segments and geographies. But you're not going to double the production capacity for data centers in the US by relying on labor alone. You're not going to reduce the cycle time to bring up a data center from four years to two years if you can't redesign the boards, redesign them for manufacturing, and manufacture them at scale in a constricted timeline.

我们真正看好的方向

What we're really bullish on is not AI completely automating away design work. It's AI being able to automate away the type of design that produces very manufacturable outputs.

We have a lot of moonshot ideas about how you can improve the robotics on the line. But the core goal is: if the design is constrained, you can manufacture it at 100% automation today. I don't need to wait for robotics to get better — the robots are already here.

主持人评论： It is funny to think about the design of a data center, with all the complex optimizations you need to do and the specs you need to fit into a particular site footprint, actually being similar — surprisingly similar — to the design of a very small circuit board that needs to fit lots of different components and meet lots of different specs. I don't think I fully appreciated that till right now.

三、把每一个软件工程师变成电气工程师？

Q: David, you've said your goal is to transform every software engineer into an electrical engineer. Why and how?

I want to re-qualify that.

Software has incredibly good properties. Empirically we are immediately observing this with tools like Claude Code — agents have been able to leverage the structured nature of code to do things that are not code-like at all. You can use Claude Code to do things that are completely orthogonal.

So the initial comment was: yes, we need to take the current set of software engineers we have in the United States and allow them to also be electrical engineers. But now the total set of software engineers — or people that are able to produce software — is exploding, and it includes agents.

What you want to do is: give anything that has the ability to generate code the same ability to generate hardware.

This will include greatly skilled SWEs building beautiful cathedrals on a PCB — a lot of our employees, whom I'm very fond of. But it will also include a lot of smaller designs that are completely automated away by allowing an agent to use code to build the board itself.

The complexity of those designs today is limited, but you can extrapolate the derivative and get to where we want to be in a year or two.

四、如何改变一个传统行业的工作方式？

Q: You're both working in industries with very entrenched expertise, ways of working, and tools — very different from software engineering where engineers are naturally technology curious. How do you change an entire industry's way of working? How much do you have to vertically integrate?

Alex: 建筑业的激励结构问题

On our side, there's a lot of "you have to earn it." It's a super traditional industry.

主持人： It's hard to think of a more traditional industry than construction.

Even manufacturing feels pro-technology and light years ahead of where the construction industry feels.

The incentive structure is helpful to understand. The source of capital sets the incentives all the way down through the project. Basically, a project you want to go build goes through a stage-gated process of removing risk so you can fund it. Eventually you get a project that feels fundable to an investor — a very different profile than venture. These are people who all they want is a stable IRR.

They want a set rate of return. I have no ability to take that going to zero, and I don't even care about capturing the upside.

All those incentives force the adoption of new technology down. When you get to someone who might have some new piece of technology, it's super unincentivized — there's no upside. No one actually wins from that environment.

It looks actually really similar to defense — in a pre-Anduril day, if you will. Someone's got budget. I'm going to pay you — like four companies to the government.

That's the process of saying, "Hey, this is just wrong. It's just broken." And that change just hasn't been able to permeate through to this industry. You'd walk into one of these firms, look at people's computers, and feel like you're stuck in the late '90s.

为什么必须垂直整合

For us, vertical integration means we have to own enough of it that we can actually do a clean interface to the industry, rather than trying to pick off one small part and then force people to change and adopt that technology. It's a process of us owning a big enough portion where we can reasonably do it as a startup — and then earn the right to continue to chunk more and more of that from a vertical integration perspective.

Q: Do you have to hire forward-leaning people? For example, you need a registered civil engineer to sign off on drawings. How do you construct the team?

Most of our team is actually not software engineers — we're mostly multi-disciplinary: mechanical, electrical, civil, lots of simulation, controls. We definitely have people with AI and software background, but in our experience it's been much easier to teach a multi-disciplinary person the latest and greatest AI tools than the other way around.

As long as you think about AI as a core primitive to how we build it into the product, it's much easier to take the domain expert to it. The good thing is there are electrical engineers who are incredibly bright and love sitting at the cutting edge of a product. We have some folks that come from large capital projects in oil and gas and energy, but the majority work on cutting-edge vehicles, rockets — that type of background.

David: 电气工程师的"品味"挑战

Q: Electrical engineers are sort of famous — no offense, since you yourself are an electrical engineer — for being fixed in their ways.

Opinionated and tasteful. That's, I think, what you're looking for.

— Tasteful, yes. — Very Italian. Very Italian of them.

This is something we had to think about very hard at the beginning of Diode, when the idea was in its infancy.

It was more: there is a pain point I am feeling myself when I go through the process of designing hardware, and there is a new technology that very clearly holds the keys to solving a lot of this pain point. If I had to give this technology to the world, how would I give it?

One somewhat obvious answer in 2020 would have been: let me build CAD software. CAD software has its own idiosyncrasies as an industry — very high switch cost, lots of reasons why building a CAD software business is complicated.

Our goal was: if you truly make the leap of faith that the models will do it themselves eventually, what you want to build is the infrastructure for the models to be able to go and do these things that they're not currently able to do. You don't want to convince people to buy your software. You want to convince people to buy the end product.

把自己卖成一家制造公司

This is a much harder company to build. It does require a much larger width of market.

Today, the way we explain ourselves to our enterprise customers — Fortune 100 companies — is not "Hey, we're going to sell you a software product." It's: you are used to working with companies that deliver the exact same product — I give you my specifications, I get back a physical product. We fit into that mold. How we do it is an implementation detail to the company. But we do it faster and we do it cheaper — which is what matters at the end of the day.

We also offer some major advantages, like introspection into how the process happens. You see in real time your board being designed and delivered — something engineers, especially software engineers, are very used to. It's like a pull request on GitHub where you see the code streaming in real time.

— The checks passing. — Yeah, exactly.

开源编译器、闭源产品

Our core compiler toolchain is open source. If any electrical engineer wants to take it and run with it — please be my guest — because once you generate an artifact, it will work in our ecosystem. We will be able to take it and send it for manufacturing.

We want to own the infrastructure, not the core design primitives. We are very bullish that those design primitives will be very helpful to both humans and agents.

That's the bet we made: the infrastructure is open source (diodecb on GitHub). We have people trying it in a closed beta, giving us feedback and using our internal tools we haven't released yet. We will release more and more tools. But the way we harness that power is by doing everything end to end.

Alex put it beautifully: you need to do enough. If you try to do too much too early, you dilute yourself and your focus becomes really complicated.

The other benefit of this strategy: a lot of the times the AI is not 100% of the way there, and you want to be able to place company-defining bets even if the product itself may not be 100% there. For example, we are 90% more efficient at building boards than we were in a world without our tools. But there's still 10% of work required, which is why people pay our current services right now. The eventual goal: as the models get better, that percentage goes down and down until it's fully self-service and you can manufacture right away.

主持人： You both touched on this — sell the thing that the customer knows how to buy.

Alex: In project finance, time literally is money. If it's a project that takes one year to build versus five years at the same cost, that's a very different IRR.

Alex: The value prop for us is not that it's cheaper. From an engineering perspective, engineering is such a small percentage of the cost that no one wants to compress that. It is that if you can bring in the schedule by three or six months, that materially impacts financeability.

五、如何让 AI 理解物理世界？数据从哪里来？

Q: How have you worked to get AI to understand real-world physics and constraints? There's no Common Crawl for construction design data, or for PCBs.

David: 把电路板问题重新编码为代码问题

Maybe I'll start because I have the easiest path. In general, my company is much easier than Alex's. I admire Alex and respect him a lot because his domain is very beautiful and complicated. I do simple things. I build electronics.

We cheat. Instead of requiring a ton of data, we reframe the problem with something there's a ton of training data on — which is code.

Traditional electronics is not designing code at all. Very tasteful electrical engineers despise the idea of writing code. And that's fine — we don't want anybody who doesn't want to write code to write it. Let the model write the code. The model is amazing at writing code. It understands the concepts.

We basically built a compiler that gives the model enough hints that it feels like it's writing a Python program instead of designing a circuit board. This is how we got around the schematics problem.

物理约束仍然存在

There are a lot of actual physics problems.

主持人： You're literally putting something on a square that has to fit into some form factor. You do have physical constraints beyond just the code.

That's exactly right. And there there's no data. You're right that there's much more data than in construction, but the data is two or three orders of magnitude smaller than what you'd need to train a foundation model to actually do the proper job.

Because this is a physics-based problem, you see companies in the space trying to tackle "how do I represent it physically." We actually see agent models being able to slowly and painfully start to do it the same way a human would.

We also have some moonshot projects in the works. Maybe we'll chat about it next time. I'll just say: I'm very bullish on diffusion as an architecture for this specific problem.

Bootstrapping 数据飞轮

You need to bootstrap it somehow. For us, the bootstrapping comes from code first — build a library of validated blocks, and those blocks become training data for the next round of models. You build compounding interest.

Also, if you become the rails that everybody can design for free on — that's data that comes your way. As long as you give enough incentives — "Hey, I'm going to manufacture in the US at a cost competitive with Asia," which is unheard of, and you can do it profitably — you will generate enough data to make the model skyrocket in accuracy.

Alex: Everything is code

Super cool to hear. Same philosophy: everything is code. If you can play in a language the models already understand, your life is a bajillion times easier.

For us, the industry really works more deliverables-based or output-focused, where everything's a snowflake — always n-of-1. That's the core fundamental problem of why it takes so long and we don't get better. We're not improving our rate of building these projects.

Our world looks much more modeled: how do you embody all the relationships into a really robust model?

Q: When you say "model," do you mean an AI model or more of an ontological model?

Ontological model — a simple representation of a complex system. Sorry, my mother tongue.

— My my my my palentie. — Yeah, did it kick in for a minute?

You basically have a combination of an environment that agents and LLMs can generate code that fits within the framework we've provided. It has the ability to use all these deterministic tools almost the same way a regular engineer would.

为什么参数化建模是关键

If you can do that in this modeled way, you have a parametric relationship to everything. What's really important is: every time you tweak and adjust, it just gets better and its resilience improves.

The biggest way this shows up for our customers: if you spend six months designing something, and you have another three months before you lock everything and fund the project and build it — if you want to change something six months in...

— You'd like start over. It's like cascade — — Literally. Yeah. It's a total nightmare.

In this version, everything's just an updated variable. It's highly optimized and you can iterate on these things.

It's basically this combination of a very modeled approach that allows you to use these agents to write code — which is what they know how to do.

— Put on rails. — Everything is code. — Everything is computer.

六、仿真在数据生成中的角色

Q: How much do you use simulation, either for data generation or other purposes?

Alex: 仿真作为计算器

We certainly use simulation to compute the values required for the design. But we don't have —

Q: Give an example.

I want to understand the mass flow rate of a fluid going through a pipe with a certain consistency at a certain temperature and pressure. That's a problem with multiple interdependent variables. You run it into a simulation environment, explore a pretty wide space, ideally goal-seeking or optimizing toward something.

That's just a calculator. We've done that simulation software for decades. Those are tools for us — you train an AI how to use that specific tool, how to run a bunch of optimizations.

You can do that across simulating how electrons move, how fluids move, how structures move when the earth shakes — all through simulation.

The good thing about our world: when you think about designing any of these big projects, it's just Legos on hard mode. You are empirically saying the only way these things fit together validly is if it's either been done before or it perfectly matches up from an inputs and outputs perspective. It is an extremely calculable answer in almost all scenarios — versus something where we have to predict how it will perform in an environment where we have no data against it.

David: 仿真作为训练时工具，而非推理时工具

I have two answers. We have a ton of simulation already because our world is made for that. We've had tools in electrical engineering like SPICE at the schematics level, and electromagnetic simulation kernels like openEMS on the open source side, or Ansys on the actual board level. Both exist and are used in the industry and are very important.

The way we currently use simulation: to give a grounding to the model if there's reinforcement learning in the loop. You can build a circuit, and it's very easy to determine whether or not that circuit is correct without relying on a golden reference. You can build it in many different ways — as long as you achieve your output, you're good. Simulation in that case is very important.

But what I'm really hopeful for: electrical engineers today do not mostly rely on pure simulation whenever they build a board. They have a really good internal intuition for why the design is done in a certain way. They almost use simulation as a way to verify that as the last step — versus as the design itself.

Alex: That's exactly right.

My eventual goal: simulation becomes a train-time tool that you use for the model to become better at developing that taste — having that intuition. At inference time, you don't want to run simulation. Simulation has some properties — it's not super fast. You can speed it up with parallel kernels, but fundamentally, simulation is something we hope to use at training time rather than inference time.

We built the hooks to do it because it's important to ground physicality — you need to provide ground truth somehow. We are really seeing emerging properties where if you train the model with enough data, it will develop that taste. In 95% of cases, that's what you want. You get the product very fast, and it's very hard to beat actual manufactured hardware.

The best engineers I know will spend three weeks simulating something, and then say "I don't trust it until I actually built it and put it in an enclosure, which will completely change my electromagnetic properties." Simulation needs to be a training tool, and then you need physics to tell you you're right or wrong. Reducing the training time is probably the most important part.

七、最后 10% 需要新的架构突破吗？

Q: David, you said you can 90% do the design, and there's still 10% where a human finishes. To close that last gap, do we need a fundamental breakthrough in ML or AI research to understand the physical world? Or is it just further scaling?

David: 这是一个哲学分歧

My co-founder Lenny is the smarter one. This is a very important premise: he and I have a philosophical disagreement on this.

I think we already have all the building blocks. My personal take: the last frontier standing is that we don't have enough data on circuit boards specifically. A lot of things you can do will work, but the data is the thing we need to generate as a society if we want circuit boards to be automated by AI.

The data exists. It's usually siloed into the Apples, Metas, SpaceXes of the world, and they will not fork it over. Individually, none of this is enough — you really need to pull it together. So either great collaboration (everybody open-sources their data — unlikely), or we find a way to produce enough data that the models get inherently better.

If a new architecture comes out — amazing, you can be more efficient with less data. But that's my current take.

Lenny 的反方立场

There is a competing take — Lenny's side. A lot of these problems are really well structured for Monte Carlo tree search / reinforcement learning style approaches. You can formulate a problem where two players play against each other and get better just by nature of improving recursively.

I usually defer to his opinion. So maybe there's a world where we don't need any more data, and things are already on the table — we just need to tickle them the right way.

In the meantime, because this is an open question, we'll focus on building the thing end to end. We'll bridge the 10% however we need to. At the current pace of improvement in both architecture and data generation capabilities, breakthroughs will happen. You need to be in a position where you benefit from them and you're not in their path.

Alex: 构造系统就是为了全自动

Q: Your area seems more "permeable" — less of a controlled system, more variables like wind. Will it require a human in the loop forever?

I think our problem — it's even more sparse. We're not an order of magnitude or two off. So we don't even really have that as an option on the table.

Most of those problems can be bounded. The benefit of this space is: there is an incredible amount of standards that govern how something should be built. There will always be optimization benefit from more data points or more nuanced information. But to beat status quo — the bar is so unbelievably low.

I would take the under on it. I think we will be at end to end.

It's an important design paradigm: making sure you design the system to actually be fully autonomous and not human in the loop. For us at least, it feels like it's driven a very different architecture. We've similarly bet that the model does get better. If it doesn't, we're maybe back to the drawing board. But the system itself needs to be designed with the requirement that this is end-to-end automated.

八、人形机器人：过热还是低估？

Q: Physical AI feels scary to a lot of people. But let's start with humanoids — a contentious topic. For David in manufacturing, and for Alex on construction sites. Overhyped, underhyped, does it matter?

Alex: 让未来感觉起来像未来

I'm super excited for the future to feel like the future. That's incredibly inspiring. I want to live in that world.

It'll definitely be a core component of us. Humanoids — for the whole —

Q: Humanoids in particular, versus fit-for-purpose robots like "I'm really good at moving lumber" or "I'm a concrete-pour robot"?

There'll totally be all the above. There's going to be broadly more automation at large. But there's a reason to centralize around a design and mass manufacture it. The efficiency you get out of manufacturing a thing — the learning rate, driving cost down — outweighs the nuanced custom efficiency.

That form factor will be very relevant for an incredible scope. And there will be specialized robotics too — the same way that when you engineer giant facilities, there are always volume-to-surface-area scaling laws that say you should customize that giant vessel rather than standardize.

David: I love all robots equally

Q: Will you have humanoids on your PCB manufacturing line?

I have the luxury of loving all robots equally. I don't care if they're humanoid or special. They all have PCBs. I love them. They have PCBs inside them. I love you.

For our specific process: electronics and circuit boards are already so automated that your goal is to bridge that gap. It can be a humanoid. I'm very bullish on VLAs. I suspect there will be a lot of improvements to the remaining 20% of work — which you can do with a robotic arm (already exists hardware-wise), with smarter ability to discriminate components and do computer vision on the fly.

一个具体例子：波峰焊

A very specific example: if you want to solder very chunky components, you have two options. You do it by hand — take a human and do it. Or you have a wave reflow oven — a huge molten pool of tin. I was in Europe visiting some manufacturers, and they told me: "It's literally so expensive to heat up the tin in terms of energy that we don't turn it on because the volume we do isn't worth it. So we'll just do it by hand."

That kind of thing feels very achievable to automate with robotics.

更大的图景：机器人必须接管脏活累活

But this is marginal in our assembly line.

What is not marginal: if you look at the implications of automating knowledge work today — at some point there's a vastly larger chunk of the economy that needs someone to physically do something. If you need to mine ore, somebody needs to mine that ore. It can be a machine or a human — hopefully not a human. Not the greatest job. You don't want to put humans in harm's way.

You want robots to do this kind of stuff. What we want to do is facilitate this. One example: actuators. We have some IP in motor controlling. I have a good friend, David Hansen, who builds beautiful motors with Western Magnetics materials, and we want to build them. We want to be part of this.

I don't know if I'd 100% bet on a specific form factor. We invest in — and really like — robots that are specific. Pick-and-place machines are basically robots. But there will be more and more and more. This is part of the bet we're making.

九、默会知识、技工荒与人口老龄化

Q: You touched on something Dan Wang calls "process knowledge" — which China has in spades, the US has some, but with an aging workforce. Manufacturing engineers, construction engineers, electrical engineers who've developed intuition and taste. How important is capturing that? Can we encode it in models, or do we need to train the next generation?

Alex: 技工才是真正的护城河

There is a lot of tacit knowledge in the industry that helps be more of a shortcut or rule of thumb to the right answer that you can otherwise first-principles derive. So there's a scenario where you can do a heck of a lot more work when your marginal cost goes to zero — it's fine, we solve the problem.

But where it really is — is in the trades. The electrician and how they work. There's an incredible amount of tacit knowledge there. That's both a challenge, and yes — we need so many more of them.

I could totally be wrong on this number, but I'd guess the average salary of an electrician in Texas right now is higher than a Silicon Valley software engineer. It's incredible. Super demanded.

主持人： I was talking to the CTO at Microsoft, and he told me that one time Microsoft employed a third of the electricians in the state of Georgia when they were building a big data center there. Just wild.

People are turning to manufacturing for a lot of the data center scope because there aren't enough people in the trades to build these projects. The only alternative is to mass-manufacture these things — concentrate labor in a modular scenario, even at a premium from a cost perspective.

It's an all-of-the-above strategy. We should totally be training more people on very practical skills that will be needed for a while. And hopefully start to codify a lot of that, so when we really want to go into scale mode — in a real world of abundance when intelligence falls to zero — it'd be great to embody that into robotics too.

David: 设计者与制造者的文化断裂

Q: Do we need more PCB manufacturing technicians?

My take: the biggest cultural disconnect — something I saw in spades when I was living in Hong Kong — is that a lot of the result of being able to just send your designs like ivory tower (you design in the US and send to manufacturer somewhere else) means you kind of don't feel the pain. The pain is really disconnected, and that's why the design-for-manufacturing muscle atrophies.

More so than the know-how on the line, it's this idea that the person who designs the board will be the same one who manufactures it — or very close, like their friends.

If you look at a lot of electrical engineering design done in China: it's designed so that it's easy to make. It's visceral.

I had this friend — I was like, "Why do you make your boards so cramped? Why not just do it on double side?"

He looks at me: "But then you have to do two passes on the SMT line."

I'm like, "But it's not you."

He says, "No, but I know the person who's going to do it. My board is going to arrive later and be more expensive. I'm just going to spend a little more time designing it."

This is very cultural. What's missing — at least in the circuit board world — is this very visceral connection. And I don't know that you can artificially manufacture it, or hire your way into it, or pull people out of retirement. The only way is through.

Claude 作为无尽耐心的 DFM 审查员

You need to find a very cheap way to generate these DFM-ready designs — and Claude doesn't care if you bash it and say "Yes, this is good, but make it more manufacturable, make it more manufacturable." Or you say: "Hey, here are 150 checks, go through all of them and painstakingly change the artifact until it's easy for me to make."

Of course, there are very smart people already. All contract manufacturers for PCBs in the US are very talented. Actually, the capabilities of contract manufacturers in the US are super high, because they only bid on military contracts which require the highest possible capabilities.

But what you want to build is a new set of mass production capabilities, which has been evaporated by the industry because the economics didn't make sense. We are betting on doing it by teaching the designer — which in this case is not necessarily a human — to do it for you.

十、收尾：二阶效应与使命

Q: Both of you talk about using AI in the physical world to do more of something — build more PCBs in the US, construct more large-scale industrial projects. How do you think about the second-order effects? Maybe this gets to the mission statement of why you're working on what you're working on.

David: 让硬件创业像 SaaS 创业一样简单

I feel this pain personally. I want to be able to spin up a hardware company the same way my friends spin up B2B SaaS. You should be able to say, "I want to do something that's considered very hard," and just go do it.

The second-order effects: today we have what is effectively the equivalent of curing cancer happening in artificial intelligence. It's this marvelous thing — if three years ago you gave somebody Claude Code, they would have thought you were a sorcerer. It is that good. But it's also so bad at actually delivering physical products.

It's marvelous because it can do it in some capacity, but we really need to have the same step-wise improvement we had for software — for physical design. So that American engineers, American teenagers, can be passionate about building physical things and say, "I want to build a CubeSat and put it in orbit and do cool things with it — and it's easy because I can just spin up and have my boards manufactured next day."

Those are the second-order effects I'm interested in. And the only way we get that is if we teach models to actually do things in the real world. This is why we started a company.

Alex: 重建"建造野心"的肌肉

For us — if you look at basically any US construction metric — labor productivity, adjusted capex numbers — over the past 50 years, we're getting worse. In my past life I come from the world of software, where de facto everything gets better, there's always progress. That is clearly not true in this space.

You extrapolate that line out, and we just lose the muscle of knowing how to build large ambitious projects. That's the graph I see when I close my eyes at the end of the night.

For us, it's: are we able to do an aspect of what this life cycle of building projects looks like — an order of magnitude better — so that we earn the right to redo the whole thing, and solve it from incentives down? How do you build an order of magnitude more from a projects perspective?

That's everything from the energy we need to actually win in AI and build all these data centers, to the advanced manufacturing companies we're doing to reindustrialize, to critical minerals. The core bones of how basically all this stuff you see when you look around works — we're getting worse at. That's a very concerning thing.

结语

主持人： This is really fun. I'm glad both of you are working on your respective problems. I'm glad you're in our portfolio. I'm leaving this conversation optimistic. Thanks so much.

— Thank you.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 一、最反直觉的观察：AI 不是在"学会"硬件，而是在"伪装"硬件为软件
表面上看，这是两家把 AI 带入物理世界的公司。但如果你仔细听 David 说的那句"我们建了一个编译器，让模型感觉自己在写 Python 程序而不是在设计电路板"——你会发现**真正的突破根本不是 AI 理解了物理世界，而是人类把物理世界重新编码成了 AI 已经懂的语言**。
这是一个极其狡猾的认知翻转：过去三十年，软件工程师一直在试图"模拟物理世界"（CAD、仿真、数字孪生）；现在这两家公司做的恰恰相反——**把物理世界"软件化"，直到它能被 LLM 当作代码消化**。Alex 那句"Everything is code. Everything is computer."不是口号，是方法论。
换句话说，**他们不是在造物理 AI，他们是在为物理世界做"语义迁移"**。数据不够？那就换一个数据充足的语言（代码）来描述问题。这是数据稀缺时代的元策略——一种智力层面的套利。
## 二、隐藏的系统联系：建筑业、硬件制造与 Pre-Anduril 时代的国防工业，本质上是同一种病
Alex 轻描淡写地提到

item.title: [Bits + Bips] Should DeFi Stop Trusting Transactions? The Answer Is Probably Yes.
item.dc:creator: Bits + Bips
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

「放弃全得」作为一种系统性成熟

嘉宾三次重复"resigned"（认命/释然），这个词的选择本身就是一次系统级的自白。DeFi 社区长期被"去信任 + 高性能 + 完全可组合 + 用户主权"的四位一体愿景驱动，而这段短短的发言实际上宣告了一个不可能三角的第四条边被正式承认。真正值得注意的不是他承认要牺牲，而是他用"认命"而非"选择"——这意味着社区的决策模式正在从"设计最优解"转向"接受约束后的次优解"，这是一种从青春期到成年期的心智转变。

意外观察：DeFi 的"信任"与金融无关，而与物理学同构

表面上这段话是在谈"是否要信任交易"，但深层结构其实和热力学第二定律惊人相似——你无法在一个封闭系统内同时最小化熵（不可逆性/信任成本）和最大化功（吞吐/可组合性）。传统金融的解法是引入"外部热源"（央行、法院、保险公司）来降低局部熵；DeFi 过去十年一直假装自己是永动机。嘉宾"认命"的那一刻，其实是在承认：DeFi 也需要一个"外部热源"，可能是预确认者（pre-confirmers）、可能是意图求解器（solvers）、也可能是被重新定义的"受托方"。区块链并没有消灭信任，它只是把信任打包、迁移、再重命名了。

跨领域连接：这是航空业 1950 年代的翻版

有一个冷门但精确的类比——1950 年代的商用航空。早期航空工程师坚信"绝对安全 + 高速 + 低成本"可以同时实现，直到 de Havilland Comet 连续空难后，整个行业"认命"地接受了冗余（redundancy）会拖慢速度、增加成本，但必须容忍。从那以后，航空业的进步不再是追求"完美飞机"，而是围绕"可接受的失败模式"进行工程化。DeFi 现在正站在同一个拐点：未来十年的赢家不会是那些声称"零信任、零延迟、零 MEV"的项目，而是那些诚实地公布自己牺牲了哪一项、并把这种牺牲设计成可审计失败模式的协议。

一个反直觉的推论：愿意公开说"我们信任某某环节"的 DeFi 项目，长期反而比声称"完全无需信任"的项目更值得信任——因为前者把风险显性化，后者把风险埋藏在了未被命名的假设里。

📝 转录文本

对现状的释然

I have resigned myself to the belief that I think that we are not going to be able to have our cake and eat it, too. And we're going to have to figure out — we're going to have to tolerate some of them.

共识：当前范式不可持续

Cuz one thing I think all of us agree on is: the current paradigm is not sustainable.

必须有所取舍

I'm so resigned to the fact that maybe it's just not possible to get the best of all worlds, and that we're going to have to sacrifice something somewhere along the line.

观看原视频 →
item.description:
## 「放弃全得」作为一种系统性成熟
嘉宾三次重复"resigned"（认命/释然），这个词的选择本身就是一次系统级的自白。DeFi 社区长期被"去信任 + 高性能 + 完全可组合 + 用户主权"的四位一体愿景驱动，而这段短短的发言实际上宣告了一个**不可能三角的第四条边被正式承认**。真正值得注意的不是他承认要牺牲，而是他用"认命"而非"选择"——这意味着社区的决策模式正在从"设计最优解"转向"接受约束后的次优解"，这是一种从青春期到成年期的心智转变。
## 意外观察：DeFi 的"信任"与金融无关，而与物理学同构
表面上这段话是在谈"是否要信任交易"，但深层结构其实和热力学第二定律惊人相似——**你无法在一个封闭系统内同时最小化熵（不可逆性/信任成本）和最大化功（吞吐/可组合性）**。传统金融的解法是引入"外部热源"（央行、法院、保险公司）来降低局部熵；DeFi 过去十年一直假装自己是永动机。嘉宾"认命"的那一刻，其实是在承认：DeFi 也需要一个"外部热源"，可能是预确认者（pre-confirmers）、可能是意图求解器（solvers）、也可能是被重新定义的"受托方"。

item.title: [Invest Like The Best] The Supply and Demand of AI Tokens | Dylan Patel Interview
item.dc:creator: Invest Like The Best
item.content:encoded:
💡 创造性洞见

一个让人意外的观察：Dylan 不是在描述 AI 革命，他是在描述一场"token 封建主义"的复辟

表面上这是关于供需的技术访谈，但如果你仔细听，会发现一个惊人的结构性真相正在浮现：我们正在从"软件的民主化时代"退回到"计算的贵族制时代"。

过去 20 年互联网的故事是"边际成本趋近于零"——软件一旦写出来，所有人平等地使用。而 Dylan 描述的世界恰恰相反： - Mythos 只给"愿意下跪求情"的人 - Ken Griffin 可以用 $10B 买断首发 token 供应 - "你必须有一个 Anthropic 的销售代表"才能拿到真正的 rate limit - 模型将"越来越少地广泛部署"

这不是 SaaS。这是中世纪的行会制度——技术的访问权重新与社会关系、资本和"被恩赐"的身份绑定。Dylan 自己在访谈里无意识地用了一个极具启示性的词："permanent underclass（永久下层阶级）"。这不是科技词汇，这是阶级分析的词汇。

跨领域连接：这其实是"石油诅咒"在数字世界的镜像，但反过来了

看一个意想不到的类比——token 经济学的结构与 20 世纪初的石油经济完全同构，但因果链是反的。

石油时代：供给有限 → 谁控制油田谁赚钱 → 洛克菲勒式垄断 → 下游用户用石油创造价值（工业、汽车）

Token 时代：需求爆炸性无限 → 谁控制 compute 谁赚钱 → Anthropic/OpenAI 式准垄断 → 下游用户用 token 创造价值

但这里有一个 Dylan 自己都没完全说透的反转：石油的价值上限由物理世界的消耗率决定（一辆车烧多少油是有限的），而 token 的价值上限由"想法的质量"决定，而想法本身在被 AI 无限生成。所以这是人类历史上第一次出现"需求曲线的斜率本身被供给侧制造出来"的商品。

这也解释了为什么 Dylan 的 Mythos 描述让他"感到害怕"——不是因为模型太强，而是因为他第一次意识到这个系统没有自然的需求天花板。这是经济学教科书里从未出现过的物种。

隐藏的系统性真相：Dylan 自己就是"Phantom GDP"悖论的活体样本

最讽刺也最深刻的一点——Dylan 描述 Malcolm 一个人做了 200 人经济学家一年的工作，创造了"Phantom GDP"概念（产出上升但 GDP 反而缩水，因为成本暴跌）。

但 Dylan 没察觉到的是：他自己的公司 SemiAnalysis 正是 Phantom GDP 的完美范例—— - 用几周时间和 $6000/天的 token 支出，替代了"100 人做了十年"的能源数据服务公司 - 这意味着原公司的 ~$500M 估值在被蒸发 - 而 SemiAnalysis 创造的替代价值，可能只反映为数千万美元的营收 - 净结果：GDP 账面上"消失"了数亿美元的经济活动，但信息价值实际上增加了

这里隐藏着一个颠覆性的宏观预测：如果 Phantom GDP 理论成立，那么 AI 越成功，官方 GDP 数据越会显示"衰退"。这可能是 Dylan 预测三个月内会有大规模抗议的真正深层原因——不是因为失业，而是因为统计系统本身正在崩溃，人们会感受到"经济在恶化"的幻觉，即便实际福利在提升。

经济学家们即将面对他们职业生涯中最大的测量危机：我们正在建造一个用 20 世纪工具无法测量的 21 世纪经济。这比 AI 抢工作更具颠覆性——它意味着整个央行、政府、投资决策的仪表盘都在慢慢失灵。

一个反直觉的推论

如果 Dylan 的模型是对的，那么最聪明的投资策略不是买 Nvidia，而是买"token 贵族身份"——即与顶级实验室的企业合同关系本身。这是一种新型的无形资产，无法在资产负债表上显示，但可能比土地、专利或品牌更有战略价值。我们正在目睹一类全新的"准产权"的诞生，它既不是财产权也不是契约权，而更像是一种"召见权"。

📝 转录文本

开场：执行变易，想法变贵

"What used to matter a lot was execution was very very [ __ ] difficult and ideas were cheap. Now ideas are cheap and plentiful but execution is very easy. So really only the good ideas are the ones that can justify the spend on super cheap implementation."

主持人： You told me this incredible story about how your own team's use of tokens has changed dramatically this year. Yeah. Retell that story and what it is teaching you about what's going on in the world.

一、SemiAnalysis 的 Token 支出曲线

Dylan： Last year we thought we were heavy users of AI. Everyone's using ChatGPT. Everyone's using Claude. Everyone's got — you know, I'm providing whatever subscriptions anyone wants — on the order of spend of like tens of thousands of dollars for our firm.

This year the spend has just skyrocketed, and it really started in late December with Opus. That included Doug, who's president — Doug Olaflin. He's very much leading the charge in the sense of non-technical people using AI for coding. He's basically pulled the whole firm slowly over time. I think he's been the leader in doing that. Obviously the engineers were using it anyways, but spend in January just started to inflect and rocket and rocket and rocket and rocket.

We signed an enterprise contract with Anthropic, and it's gone to the point where now — I think when I last talked to you it was $5 million spend rate. It's actually $7 million spend right now.

主持人： That was last week, by the way.

Dylan： A lot of that is just the usage, right? People who have never coded before are using Claude Code and spending thousands of dollars sometimes a day. But across a firm, we're spending $7 million a year now on Claude Code at the current rate, versus our salary expense being in the neighborhood of $25 million. So we're north of 25% of spend on Claude Code as a percentage of salary. And if this trajectory continues, then we'll spend more than 100% by the end of the year — which is a bit terrifying.

Thankfully, I don't have to decide between people and AI because our company's growing so fast. It's more so like, "Okay, well, I don't have to hire nearly as fast, and I can spend a lot more on AI and it works, and we just grow faster." But I think other folks will start to reckon with the fact that, "Huh, if this person can do the work of five to ten to fifteen people using Claude Code, then all of a sudden I should probably cut people."

二、具体案例：Claude Code 正在重写工种的定义

案例 1：Oregon 反向工程实验室

But right now, I think the use cases are so broad. For example, one thing is we have a reverse engineering lab in Oregon that we've been building for a year and a half. We have a bunch of fancy microscopes, scanning electron microscopes. The whole purpose of this is you reverse engineer chips. You get the architecture out of it, you get the materials that they're using to manufacture, and this is some of the data we sell. This is a very slow process of analyzing that data.

Instead, one person on the team — they've been able to spend with a couple thousand dollars of Claude tokens — they've been able to create this application that is GPU accelerated, runs on a server that we have at CoreWeave. Anytime we send it an image, it's able to take the picture of the chip and overlay where every single material is: "Oh, this part is copper. Oh, this part of the gate is tantalum. This part of the gate is germanium. This part of the gate is cobalt." And so you can do a finite element analysis of the entire stackup of the chip very, very quickly — visual, with a dashboard GUI, everything. A few thousand dollars would — it took Claude — the person previously worked at Intel and he said that was an entire team's job to build that and maintain that. Now rack that up across the entire firm. It's insane.

案例 2：Malcolm 与 "Phantom GDP"

Another example that I think is super fun is Malcolm, who's an economist at a major bank before. Their economist department was like 100 or 200 people. What he built was the most incredible thing ever. He piped all of this different data — FRED data and all these other data, employment reports and all these other things from various APIs. We signed a couple contracts with folks to get API access to data. Pulled it all in, started running regressions, started looking at the impact of various economic revolutions on the economy from a deflationary/inflationary perspective.

The BLS — Bureau of Labor Statistics — has this entire set of like 2,000 tasks. And so he did that with AI: which ones can be done by AI, which ones cannot, and grading them across a rubric. About 3% are doable now with AI. He's created this metric so that you can measure things that can be done by AI, what the massive deflationary cost of being able to do those with AI, and therefore the deflationary aspect of it. Output can go up — it's called phantom GDP is what he called it.

Phantom GDP. Output can go up, but because cost falls so much, actually GDP theoretically shrinks. So he created this whole analysis and a brand new benchmark of language models — a set of evals across 2,000 different evals.

主持人： This all by himself.

Dylan： This is all by himself. Yeah. And he's like, "Dude, this would have taken a team of 200 economists a year." He's just completely cracked out on Claude. He's like, "Everything has changed."

三、商业逻辑：为什么不踩刹车？

主持人： How do you think about, as a business owner, going from close to zero to 25% accelerating towards whatever percent of total spend? At what point are you like, "Whoa, I need to put the brakes on this and be careful how much we're spending"? Maybe we don't need to spend on the most cutting — on Opus 4.7, which came out today. Maybe I can throttle it back to something that's a little bit cheaper.

Dylan： Ultimately, I'm in the information business, right? We sell analysis, we sell, we do consulting, we create data sets. I don't see why this wouldn't be completely commoditized on a pretty rapid basis if I'm not constantly improving.

My first product that I was selling as a data set — actually, there's more people trying to do it now. We've made it constantly better and better and better and more detailed, and so therefore it sells a market. But the way we were doing it in 2023 is not terribly different than what everyone else is doing now. If I don't move up the bar, then I will be commoditized. If I don't move fast enough, I will also lose my edge.

So the question is: yes, AI commoditizes things just like it commoditizes software. Those who can move fast and keep control of their customers and keep providing them an awesome service and keep improving the service won't shrink. They'll grow. They'll grow faster. Those who are incumbent and not doing anything — they're going to lose. So it's a bit of an existential like: if I don't adopt AI, someone else will and they will beat me.

案例 3：能源市场的闪电战

Another easy example is the energy space. We've had a few energy analysts for a year now. We've been trying to build out this energy model — it's very complex. Energy's data services market is something like $900 million. So obviously a huge market for me to try and break into, but we really hadn't broken into the energy data services business despite a year of having multiple people on the team.

Then Claude Code psychosis hits one of the people who leads the data center energy and industrial sort of business at SemiAnalysis — Jeremy — hits him. And now all of a sudden, in 3 weeks — he was spending like $6,000 a day, an insane amount — he scraped every single power plant in the US, every single transmission line above a certain voltage, and created this entire mapping of the entire US grid as well as a lot of demand sources, all from various public sources of data. We built this dashboard where you can view and check, see all the micro regions of the US where there's power deficits and surpluses.

All of these details built in a handful of weeks. We started showing some of our customers who buy our data center data set but are energy traders. They're like, "Wow, how long did this take you? This is really good. This is better than XYZ company." And then we dig deeper: XYZ company has 100 people and have been working on this for a decade.

Obviously our thing is not fully as robust, but in some ways it is better. I'm going to commoditize these energy services companies, data services companies. Who's going to commoditize me if I don't move faster? And so the question from a business owner's perspective is: yeah, I'm spending a lot, but what does that spend get me? Is it getting more revenue? Yeah.

[广告分段：Ramp / WorkOS / Felix by Rogo]

Most software companies try to maximize your time on their app to juice engagement. Ramp does the exact opposite... To see what happens when you eliminate the busy work, check out ramp.com/invest.

OpenAI, Cursor, Anthropic, Perplexity and Vercel all have something in common. They all use WorkOS... Visit workos.com to get started.

Felix by Rogo is a personal finance agent that turns a single prompt into finished client-ready work... Learn more at rogo.ai/felix.

四、信息服务业会被投资方吃掉吗？

主持人： Are you at all worried that, in the limit, the people that control capital and investing capital — who are often hiring you for what you do — will just say, "Well, we have analysts too who are really smart about this. We'll just build this ourselves"? If it's getting that easy, at what point does it all pool into the investment firms that stand to gain the most because they have the most leverage on top of the data or the insights that they glean?

Dylan： First of all, any information services business — obviously I don't generate as much value as my customer does from such information. Because if I sell you information for a dollar, you're only buying it for a dollar because you know that information helps you make a decision that lets you make more than $1. And so therefore, you have made more money off of me than I did from the information myself.

An investment fund — these investment funds all have their own information services, especially the Jane Streets of the world and the Citadels. They're really detailed on their data. And yet these sort of folks also purchase data from us and continue to do so and continue to grow with us, because I think there's just some "it" factor, right? We move faster, we're more nimble, we're a smaller team focused on just one specific thing — AI infrastructure and the huge revolution that causes in AI and tokenomics and all these things. And we really see where it's headed, and so we're moving faster and building faster.

Investment professionals — yes, they'll try and build some of the stuff we do, but more likely they'll just buy the data from us. It's cheaper for them to buy the data from us and then build on top of it than it is to build it themselves. But ultimately some may try.

五、Token 的供需经济学

主持人： I feel like every conversation I have with you, what I'm always getting at is just supply and demand of tokens. That's the thing that's interesting to me in the world right now. What has this experience taught you about the demand? Has it changed your view on the demand side of that equation, just feeling it viscerally yourself?

Dylan： If we take a step back and look at the macro lens, Anthropic has gone from $9 billion revenue to what they're at — $35–40 billion now. Probably by the time this airs, $40–45 billion ARR. Their compute has not grown to the same degree.

If you do the calculations and you assume they didn't decrease their research and development compute — they clearly didn't; their release, they have Mythos, they have Opus 4.7 — so they clearly didn't decrease their research compute spend. Ultimately, even if you assume all incremental compute they've gotten has gone towards inference, their margins are at a floor of 72%. In reality, some of that incremental compute probably went to research and development. It may be higher than 72% gross margins.

To be clear, at the start of the year, there was a leak by someone from their funding round docs — 30-something% gross margins. Where on earth does a business like this grow margins like that? In principle, their demand is so high, they're able to cut back on usage limits, rate limits, all these things. What really matters is having an Anthropic rep and having an enterprise contract with them and getting the rate limit increases that you need. Because otherwise, tokens are ultimately super in demand. Whoever can pay for them —

Anthropic has the same problem, right? Not problem — it's just the reality of how capitalism works. Yes, people are sending them $40 billion ARR in tokens, but those tokens are generating way more than $40 billion in value. Various businesses will have different value generation per token. But as we get more and more intelligent, what really matters is access to these most intelligent tokens and leveraging them.

You, as a person, deciding what is the best way to leverage these tokens to grow business and generate value — because a lot of folks will want tokens and generate tokens. But the shitty SaaS startup in SF who is using Claude to generate their software product is not necessarily actually creating a ton of value, and therefore they're going to get priced out of tokens soon enough.

关于 Mythos 与"跪求模型"的小插曲

主持人： Are you at all surprised? I had this experience just today — on the flight here I got rate limited out on something. I saw 4.7 came out and what I immediately wanted was to be on 4.7 that second. I couldn't think about using 4.6 anymore. This 4.7 is out. I was perfectly happy with 4.6 for the last many weeks. It's amazing. Are you surprised that people are so insistent on going to the most expensive leading edge thing to the degree they are?

Dylan： Without a doubt. One of my funniest memories in the past month and a half is myself and a buddy of mine, Leopold, being on our knees in front of an Anthropic co-founder begging him for access to Mythos — and then pretending it doesn't exist because we knew it existed. We were like, "Please give us access." And he's like, "I don't know what you're talking about."

主持人： What was your reaction to that rate card or that eval card coming out?

Dylan： It was rumored in the Bay Area. Everyone sort of knew it was supposed to be really good. But if you just look at the benchmarks — and obviously benchmarks change over time — Mythos is potentially the biggest step up in model capabilities in like 2 years. It's so good that they don't want to release it, even though they already announced the price to their people that they did a selective release for cyber — it's like 5 or 10x the token cost. They just don't want to release it because they're worried about the impact on the world. They're releasing a worse version of Opus 4.7 to us, and they explicitly said in the model card, "Hey, we actually preferentially made it worse at cyber." I don't know if you read that.

Whoever you are, if you have enough capital, you should get a freaking enterprise Anthropic subscription where you pay per token, not with these subscriptions, because then you won't get rate limited much. And then you need to figure out how to leverage those tokens to the highest value task and make money off of it. Because ultimately what you're doing — maybe like a year from now or two years from now — the business is actually just arbitraging tokens, right? The tokens are amazing, but let's figure out what direction to point them in. And then three or four years from now, the model will know what to do with the tokens and how to make the most value.

Token 成本曲线

You can look at this retroactively. Pick any benchmark. The cost to hit a certain capability tier used to cost X, and now it costs 1/100th or 1/1,000th of that. DeepSeek, for example, on GPT-4 was 1/600th the cost. And since then, the costs have fallen further for GPT-4 class models.

Of course, no one gives a crap about GPT-4 class models. They want the frontier, because the frontier lets them create the economically valuable things. But GPT-4 class models can still be used in stuff, so people are using them in some tiny use cases. The costs have fallen so fast — it's not really what's driving the demand. What's driving the demand is all these new use cases.

Current 4.6 Opus or 4.7 Opus tier models — a year from now, my spend for the same exact quality of the model would probably be 1/100th. I bet you it'll be 100 times cheaper — irrelevant, because I'm going to be using a way, way, way better model which can do way better things.

Anthropic's Mythos is more expensive as a model, but it spends a lot less tokens to do the thing, and therefore it is actually cheaper in most tasks than 4.6 Opus because it's just way more efficient — even though each individual token is smarter.

六、为什么 Mythos 让人"有点害怕"

主持人： When I last saw you, Mythos had just come out, maybe the day before, or the card had just come out, and you said something like — it actually made you feel like a little scared, it was so good. What did you mean by that?

Dylan： Anthropic's whole goal in 2025 — and even a lot of 2024 — was: "Hey, by the end of 2025 we need an L4 software engineer in our model." They by and large achieved that with 4.6 Opus. What they didn't say is that if you look at Mythos, and if you compare the benchmarks, it's like an L6 engineer. L4 is like pretty new. L6 is quite well experienced. I think Anthropic said that the model internally was available in February. So in two months they've gone from L4 engineer to L6 engineer. What's next?

When you think about the model progress, it's only accelerated. Anthropic's release cadence has compressed. OpenAI's release cadence has compressed. Why? Because these models generally — to make a better model — you need a few things:

  1. Amazing compute. Compute is very expensive, and it has a time scale that we track. It's growing, but it's sort of set in stone for the short term. There will be delays and shifts, and somehow you can find a little more, but it's generally pretty set in stone.
  2. Amazing researchers that people are paying tens of millions of dollars for.
  3. Implementation. Historically it has been very difficult. If I have an idea, now I have to implement it. Implementing is hard.

Now ideas are there. Implementation is very easy. It's expensive, but it's very easy. So how does one decide what ideas to implement? If your implementation is just so much easier now, you can just implement more ideas and move on the treadmill faster and faster. Whether that is AI model research — and so now your model release cadence has shrunk down to 2 months from where it was 6 months before — or, "Hey, I want to take every power plant in the US and every transmission line and model it and run regressions and see the micro supply and demand." I can also do that.

The idea is cheap. Which idea makes sense, which idea is worth the capital that you have to spend on the tokens — because the implementation is there. That's the key learning. And if implementation costs continue to tank, which they are — we don't even have Mythos yet. It's only been a handful of hours since Opus 4.7 launched, but my team is pretty excited about it internally.

What now comes to the world — it's a complete reordering of how economies work. What used to matter a lot was execution was very, very [ __ ] difficult, and ideas were cheap. Now ideas are cheap and plentiful, but execution is very easy. So really only the good ideas are worth — are the ones that can justify the spend on super cheap implementation.

恐惧的本质：资源集中化

主持人： So are you actually scared, or is it just — does it just introduce an uncertainty that's hard to grapple with?

Dylan： Uncertainty is there. But I do think that causes some fear in terms of: how does society reform itself? How does one exist in a world where your ability to implement something is not actually that important? Your ability to choose the correct idea for AI to implement, and then your ability to sell that idea or sell what the AI has implemented, is what matters. Your ability to garner capital towards that is what matters.

Going back to the point of: it's very important to have the newest model always. Who's going to have access to the newest model? Anthropic's project — I know it's not called Earwig, but I troll Anthropic people by calling it Earwig. Glasswig, Anthropic Earwig — where they only release Mythos to certain companies for cyber. That's just going to be something that continues. Models will have less broad and less broad deployment.

I know OpenAI and Anthropic and all these people are like, "We want to have great AI for everyone." AI is very [ __ ] expensive. Who's going to pay for the trillion dollars of infrastructure? People who have money and can build useful things with AI. And then you don't want people to distill your models, so you don't release them broadly. You release them to a fewer and fewer set of customers. Those customers are also now wrestling over the tokens — unless Anthropic jacks them. They could double their pricing on Opus, and I would continue to pay, and I bet most users would continue to pay.

I bet that wouldn't solve their humongous capacity problem that they have. So then the question becomes: where does this cycle end, where token usage — and therefore the benefits of those tokens, the additional value generated on top of those tokens — aggregates among fewer and fewer and fewer companies?

I don't have Mythos. You know who has Mythos? Top freaking banks. Now they're only using it for cybersecurity. But at some point I can envision a world where — because I have an enterprise Anthropic contract and because Anthropic people kind of like me, they're willing to give us slightly earlier access or slightly higher rate limits or something for a model. I hope that's what happens. And then my competitor, whoever that is, doesn't have that, and I'm able to [ __ ] crush them.

There are people — Ken Griffin of Citadel is super well-connected and super rich. He just signs — who knows? He goes and signs a deal with OpenAI/Anthropic: "Yeah, I'm going to get access to your models, and I'll buy the first $10 billion worth of tokens each year. So whenever you release the model, I'll spend the first 10 billion tokens, and then everyone else can get the model after that." And it's like, okay, well, now what does that do? Well, now he's going to crush everyone in the market.

That's just an example. Could be cyber, like Anthropic is worried about — "Oh, now I can hack people." Could be information services business like myself, where I crush someone else. It's such a broad base. We don't know what these models can do. Anthropic doesn't know what these models can do. No one knows what these models can do. It's up to the end user to figure out where they can leverage the tokens to see what they can build and imagine — which is tremendously productive and uplifting for humanity. But then what happens to the concentration of resources and usage of it?

七、机器人：第二条需求曲线？

主持人： Presumably right now robotics or robots consume relatively zero tokens versus everything else. What's your view of that? If that's like a second demand curve that could start to ratchet — there's a new startup every single day within a mile of here trying to build something interesting in robotics.

Dylan： There's this concept of software-only singularity — that the world has an AI singularity, but only in software. And now what about the rest of the world? The vast majority of the world is physical. You can see the world orient around hardware, not software. That's actually why I think software-only singularity is like just a blip, and we do get everything else. Because once software is super easy —

What makes robots really hard is programming microcontrollers and actuators and controlling all this stuff. It's very difficult right now. The interesting thing about AI models is they're actually really inefficient in learning. It's just we're able to give them so much data that they're able to learn and surpass us in certain ways.

Robots currently — the robot models, VLAs (vision-language-action models), which is very popular right now — is probably not going to be the thing that ultimately scales beyond. They are inefficient in data, and we can't scale the data for them fast enough. There is going to be some way to large-scale pre-train robot models, where just like humans see all this data throughout their lives — and what's interesting is, the reason why humans are so good is we're sample efficient. One example, two examples, we're good. Applying that to robotics —

Once you have this software-only singularity, implementation is super cheap. Anyone can start to build these models — people can start to build models that now robots are actually useful. And so I think in the next six to 18 months we'll start seeing real breakthroughs in robotics that enable few-shot learning. I.e., there's a pre-trained robot model, and now there's a robot that you have hired or bought or whatever. You show it a few examples and it's able to do it. You tell it to stack these two things, or you tell it, "Hey, this can balance perfectly," and it starts doing these things.

主持人： Nicely done.

Dylan： One shot.

主持人： No, trust me, I've spilled many a times.

Dylan： So I think robots will get few-shot learning. Right now, there's a lot of companies doing robots for advertisement or simple stuff, but it'll be like, "Oh, folding clothes." But it's going to get really niche — like robots just for cleaning chalkboards. And it's a rental service, or it'll be a model package that you download onto your standard robot that then does that. And you pay for that. Anyways, there will be a huge explosion in physical good acceleration and deflationary effects there. So that's ultimately going to keep token demand going crazy. I don't think token demand slows down personally.

八、Mythos 告诉我们：Scaling Laws 仍然有效

主持人： Did you learn anything else about the world based on Mythos's results and how it was built? My way of asking — if you break down the components of the scaling laws —

Dylan： Mythos is a materially larger model than prior models, so yes, it is a much larger model. Now, whether or not it's what chip it's trained on is not really relevant. It's the scale. And obviously, 100,000 Blackwells is equivalent to hundreds of thousands of prior generation chips. TPUs and Trainium have their different release cadence, so it's not exactly mirrored one-to-one. But ultimately, yes, Mythos is a significantly larger model. It's proof that the scaling laws still work. Everything about it shows the trend line continues of models — more compute into model makes model better.

And along the whole way, it's not just more compute into model makes model better — along the whole way we're also getting these compute efficiency wins, which is all this research compute that the labs are spending actually turning into: if I want X capability tier model every 6 months — or every two months — that cost is dramatically decreasing. But then if I scale it up massively, I get a humongous capability jump as well. So yes, it's proof that this is still happening.

Google and Anthropic are not heavy, heavy users of GPUs on the training side, but OpenAI will start having their new class of models. I think they're taking a more sensible, principled approach to scaling — in small steps. Anthropic really went for a huge jump. We'll see better and better models throughout the year, and the release cadence is only going to get faster.

九、OpenAI 没被"埋葬"：compute-bound 的平行竞赛

主持人： We've gone a long way in the conversation with saying almost nothing about OpenAI, which would have been so strange.

Dylan： So this is the interesting thing. Everyone's like, "Okay, so Anthropic's just won, right? They had Mythos in February. They never even released it because they didn't feel the need to. They're already sold out. Their revenue is already adding $10 billion a month." And then you've got Opus 4.7 today, all before OpenAI's alleged Spud release, which media such as The Information and others have posted about. So clearly Anthropic is in the lead, right, and OpenAI is cooked.

What's interesting is, because Anthropic has such bounds on compute and they can only grow it so fast — sort of to the point where Dario used to gloat about how OpenAI was being too aggressive on compute and Anthropic was more sensible in their scaling — and now Anthropic is like, "[ __ ], we should have — I wish we had a lot more compute." OpenAI is able to pay the bills perfectly fine. In fact, they've raised a ton of money to get incremental compute, in addition to the irresponsible levels of compute that they were buying from Oracle and CoreWeave and SoftBank and all these people — and Microsoft — and Trainium. Now they're getting Trainium as well from Amazon. So they've done this insane thing on compute and they know they need more.

But what's interesting is, if you were to say Opus 4.6 — let's ignore models getting better over time. Let's just take diffusion of this technology. You and I may jump on the model immediately day one, but other businesses take time, and it takes time for people to learn. And the spark of "oh [ __ ], Claude psychosis" moment doesn't hit everyone at the same time. And so by the end of the year, let's say a 4.6 Opus tier model, the economy would spend $100 billion on. I don't think that's unreasonable. It's spending $40 billion right now.

主持人： That's like a linear extrapolation.

Dylan： It's a linear extrapolation, not an exponential. To get the exponential, you need the better models. Anthropic won't have enough compute to do that. And so presumably OpenAI and Google will hit that tier soon enough. Whoever hits that tier next — sure, Anthropic may get to charge 70-plus% gross margins, but if OpenAI hits it next, they charge 50% gross margins. They still get all of this incremental demand. And probably they also won't have enough compute to serve all the users.

So, sure, maybe Mythos is a model where, if the world had enough compute, it'd be $500 billion of revenue or something crazy. There is such demand for these tokens and such limitations on compute — and we see this with H100 prices skyrocketing and the useful life of these GPUs continuing to extend. It's pretty clear even the tier 2 lab is going to be sold out of tokens, let alone the tier 1 lab. The tier 1 lab will have better margins, but the tier 2 lab will be sold out, and probably the tier 3 lab will also be close to sold out.
